data.title: In South Korea, vouchers to curb county’s falling population draw new residents
data.description:
A novel approach by South Korea to stop its rural areas from dying by paying people to live there appears to be working.
Okcheon in North Chungcheong province saw its population grow by almost 1,000 in just over two weeks since it became a participant in the state-backed programme earlier this month, according to The Korea Herald newspaper.
The farming community was officially designated a “population decline area” in 2021. By the following year, its population fell to below the 50,000 threshold, making it a top contender for the “rural basic income” programme.
Nine other communities were selected, including Yeoncheon in Gyeonggi province, Chungseon in Gangwon province and Namhae in South Gyeongsang province.
Under the scheme, residents will receive 150,000 won (US$104) in local vouchers per month for two years. No proof of employment is necessary.
For a four-person household, the benefit will total 14.4 million won over two years – a significant sum given that annual income in farming communities has stagnated at about 10 million won for the past 30 years, according to a 2023 paper by the National Agricultural Cooperative Federation.

The stipend proved irresistible at a time when a widening wage gap is forcing young people from regional areas to flock to Seoul. Those who moved between 2022 and 2023 saw their average annual income rise by 22.8 per cent, The Korea Herald reported, citing a Ministry of Data and Statistics survey released earlier this month.
From December 3, when Okcheon was named one of 10 pilot regions for the programme, to December 19, 976 people made the county their registered home, according to Yonhap news agency.
This pushed the number of new residents for the year up to 1,038, a 10-fold increase from the same period last year. On average, about 70 people a day registered Okcheon as their new address over the period this December.
Okcheon’s population, which was 48,427 at the end of last December, has risen to 49,227 as of December 22.
“The number of people moving in began to increase last month, and since the announcement, inquiries and applications have increased rapidly,” an Okcheon official told the Maeil Business Newspaper. “The moving-in window is expected to be crowded for the time being.”
With the rapid population growth, Okcheon is facing new headaches, the biggest of which is cost.


The central government has pledged to cover 40 per cent of the voucher costs, but provincial and local governments will have to cover the rest.
Okcheon will have to find about 53 billion won in funding in the next two years – about 30 per cent of the county’s entire budget. More than 3.5 billion won is needed to cover this month’s new residents alone.
The county council is now planning ways to identify fake residents who only change addresses without moving in. Measures against these “paper residencies” include on-site checks and oversight by a committee of village heads.
“We see the increase as a clear expectation effect from the basic income,” a county official told The Korea Herald. “But our focus is making sure it leads to real settlement, not temporary registration.”

data.title: Malaysia’s Najib found guilty on abuse of power charge in final 1MDB case
data.description:
Malaysia’s jailed former prime minister Najib Razak was found guilty on one count of abuse of power in the final criminal case linked to the multibillion-dollar 1Malaysia Development Berhad (1MDB) scandal, following a seven-year trial.
Justice Collin Lawrence Sequerah is still reading the remaining verdicts.
Prosecutors say that Najib abused his position to siphon 2.3 billion ringgit (US$490 million) from the scandal-hit state fund.
In total, Najib, 72, faces four charges of abusing his position to obtain the monies in gratification linked to 1MDB funds, along with 21 additional counts of money laundering.
The decision is being delivered at the Palace of Justice in Putrajaya.
The former leader could face maximum jail terms of between 15 and 20 years on each charge, as well as a fine of up to five times the value of the alleged misappropriations.
Najib is currently held at Kajang Prison, where he is more than halfway through a reduced six-year sentence for a conviction in a separate case involving a former 1MDB subsidiary.
Dressed in a navy suit, Najib sat in the dock on Friday, mostly looking down, with his children and wife, Rosmah Mansor, seated behind him.

Throughout the trial that stretched over seven years, Najib’s defence team, led by senior lawyer Muhammad Shafee Abdullah, denied any of his client’s wrongdoing and maintained that the funds were legitimate political donations from Saudi Arabia.
His lawyers said Najib had been deceived by fugitive financier Low Taek Jho, also known as Jho Low, whom they described as the “main architect” of the financial scandal.
But the court on Friday said the evidence showed an unmistakable bond between Najib and Low.
“The evidence clearly points to the fact that this was no coincidence but was evident of a relationship in which Jho Low operated as a proxy or agent of [Najib] with regard to the running of the affairs of 1MDB,” Judge Collin Lawrence Sequerah said.
“The attempt where Najib to distance [himself] from Jho Low rang hollow in the light of the overwhelming evidence of these prosecution witnesses.”
Sequerah also said that Najib’s failure to report Low amounted to condonation, given the power he wielded at the time.
“[Najib] was no country bumpkin but was possessed of not only an impeccable family and political pedigree but of superior intelligence given his academic background and his previous experience in politics and in the administration, not only regionally but also on a national level,” the judge said.
A career politician, Najib entered frontline politics at the age of 23 after returning from studies in London to contest the Pekan parliamentary seat in his home state of Pahang following the death of his father, Abdul Razak Hussein, Malaysia’s highly respected second prime minister, in 1976.
He became a deputy minister at 25, later served as Pahang chief minister and defence minister before assuming office as Malaysia’s prime minister in 2009.
Low, who remains at large, is wanted by authorities in Malaysia, the United States and Singapore.

US investigators have estimated that more than US$4.5 billion was siphoned from 1MDB through a web of fraudulent shell companies involving corrupt officials and their associates. More than US$1 billion allegedly flowed into bank accounts owned by Najib, who helped to establish the fund in 2009.
Stolen money was traced to luxury real estate in New York and Los Angeles, a superyacht once owned by Low and high-value artworks by artists including Monet and Van Gogh.
At the height of the scandal, police and anti-corruption officers raided properties linked to Najib and his family, seizing luxury handbags, jewellery and watches worth hundreds of millions of dollars and cash in foreign currencies.
The looted billions were also used to finance The Wolf of Wall Street, starring Leonardo DiCaprio, who later returned artworks and other gifts tied to the case after US authorities launched asset recovery proceedings.
In October, Malaysia’s government said more than 20 billion ringgit was recovered through settlements with financial institutions, including a multibillion-dollar agreement with Goldman Sachs, which in 2020 admitted to bribing officials to secure bond deals for 1MDB.
The corruption scandal erupted in 2015 at the height of Najib’s premiership after investigative reports revealed billions of dollars missing from the state fund.
The saga ultimately contributed to the collapse of his government in a shock election defeat in 2018, ending six decades of rule by the Barisan Nasional coalition.
The scale of the alleged losses outraged Malaysians and fuelled public anger over corruption and elite excess at a time of rising living costs.


Last October, Najib issued a rare public apology over the scandal, saying it pained him “every day” that the 1MDB debacle occurred on his watch as prime minister and finance minister, though he denied direct responsibility.
“I deeply regret what has taken place at SRC and 1MDB,” he said in a statement. “However, being held legally responsible for things that I did not initiate or knowingly enable is unfair to me and I hope and pray that the judicial process will, in the end, prove my innocence.”
Najib was found guilty of corruption and money laundering in 2020 in a separate case involving funds from SRC International, a 1MDB unit. Two years later, he became the first Malaysian prime minister to be jailed after exhausting all appeals.
Earlier this week, a Malaysian court rejected Najib’s house arrest bid, saying a supposed addendum by former king Sultan Abdullah Ahmad Shah had no legal force as it was not deliberated by the Pardons Board.
Najib, who has been imprisoned since 2022, had his original 12-year jail sentence halved last year following a partial royal pardon.

data.title: Where to ring in 2026 in Hong Kong, from the Mandarin Oriental and Kimpton, to jazz at Zuma
data.description:
It’s been a long and eventful 2025 – and whether you’re ready to welcome 2026 with open arms, or just welcome an excuse to let your hair down, we’ve rounded up a selection of some of Hong Kong’s best nightlife venues for ringing in the New Year.
The Aubrey: After Dark New Year’s Eve Countdown Party

The Mandarin Oriental’s upscale cocktail and izakaya concept brings us a special edition of its Aubrey After Dark parties this December 31. Curated by prominent local nightlife figures Marina Bullivant and Andrew Bull, the countdown party will feature headline sets by DJs Willer and Belloy.
Where: 25/F, Mandarin Oriental, 5 Connaught Road Central
Price: HK$988 (between 10pm and midnight), HK$500 (after midnight)
Terrible Baby: Back to the Future

Over on Kowloon side, Eaton HK and Terrible Baby are doubling down on the New Year’s temporal theme. From 9pm to 2am, Terrible Baby is putting the night in the hands of DJs Fergus, Tedman Lee and Vegan Chicken, in addition to four-piece band Misty Blue Banana.
Where: 4/F, Eaton HK, 380 Nathan Road, Jordan
Price: HK$550 (early bird, until December 28), HK$630
Gossip: The Last Secret of the Year

Socio’s sister venue and nightlife concept promises one last secret and reveal for the year. Starting from 11pm, Gossip’s New Year’s Eve party will feature an exclusive show by DJ Drako as he spins his signature blend of Korean mash-ups and hip-hop.
Where: 3/F, Foco, 48 Cochrane Street, Central
Price: HK$350, including one standard drink
Swim Club: New Year’s Eve Hollywood Sock Hop

If it’s a fresh view of Victoria Harbour you’re looking for to ring in 2026, look no further than Swim Club at the Kimpton. Specially for the occasion, the venue promises “a rooftop scene of neon gloss and retro cool”, complete with gliding rollergirls and rockabilly beats from live DJs. Easy bites like tacos, sliders and grilled skewers will keep guests fuelled alongside free-flow cocktails, beers, wine and, of course, champagne.
Where: Kimpton Tsim Sha Tsui, 11 Middle Road, Tsim Sha Tsui
Price: HK$888 (early bird), HK$1,288
The Trilogy: Golden Countdown

The triple-threat nightlife concept – comprising nightclub Keyz, rooftop bar Wav and jazz bar Ella – atop H Code is pulling out all the stops to finish 2025 with a bang. No surface of the rooftop concept will be without gold – from platters to confetti – as resident DJ Anil Ahuja takes to the decks, to be joined by a surprise guest DJ later in the night.
Where: 26/F, H Code, 45 Pottinger Street, Central
Price: HK$338 (online), HK$388 (at the door)
Zuma: New Year’s Eve Countdown Party

Zuma will be blue turning grey (full marks to those who recognise the jazz reference) as the upscale Japanese concept offers a Yashoku night brunch (from 7.30pm, at HK$1,980), before transforming into a jazz club featuring sets by a live band and Zuma’s resident DJ – all enhanced by free-flow Ruinart champagne.
Where: 5-6/F, Landmark Atrium, 15 Queen’s Road Central
Price: HK$1,080

data.title: 5 of the best things to do in Hong Kong this weekend, December 26-28
data.description:
The holidays are in full swing, and as the festivities reach their peak, numerous venues in Hong Kong are ready for celebration.
Whether you are drawn to the buzz of harbourside markets, the tranquillity of nature or the wonder of a curated exhibition space, the city offers a wide range of options to help you embrace the season’s spirit.
Here are five things to do in Hong Kong this weekend.
1. AIA Carnival
The AIA Carnival returns to Central Harbourfront this season, celebrating its 11th year and running until March 1. In addition to its extensive line-up of rides and games, which include new attractions like an 80-metre-high (262 feet) Big Ben Tower swing ride, the event incorporates a wider festive programme.
Visitors can enjoy a winter market hosting food stalls from heritage brands and local favourites, watch acrobats from around the world at a winter-themed circus and listen to live music at themed pop-ups such as Blue Girl Dai Pai Dong. The carnival grounds will also host interactive art installations, wellness workshops and a quiet sensory space specifically for the neurodivergent community.
Tickets and further details are available through the official website.
2. WestK Christmas Lane












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by WestK 西九文化區 (@westk.hk)



While WestK Christmas Town is offering a long list of festive events and performances, food definitely takes centre stage. The pet-friendly Christmas Lane on Harbourside East Lawn has set up 30 local food and retail stalls, making it the perfect opportunity to savour seasonal gourmet specials against the backdrop of Victoria Harbour.
The offerings include Mother of Pizzas, Pane e Latte, PostCoffee and local selections such as Ah Jeng’s Coffee Club. Several vendors will also provide pet-friendly treats and photography services.
While the overall Christmas Town will run until January 11, Christmas Lane will only be available until December 31.
3. Kadoorie Farm and Botanic Garden

For families looking for festivity outside the city, Kadoorie Farm and Botanic Garden (KFBG) is offering a natural escape this holiday season. The conservation centre is offering free admission for children aged 11 and under until December 31, providing a perfect opportunity for a gentle retreat into nature.
Visitors can explore the newly renovated Piers Jacobs Wildlife Sanctuary to see rescued animals like barking deer and leopard cats in enriched habitats, as well as a botanical art exhibition showcasing Hong Kong’s native flora through illustrations and Chinese paintings.
For hands-on fun, families can join interactive workshops such as a Fine Brush Plant Painting session for ages six and up, or become nature detectives in the Plant Encounters garden activity.
For more information and ticketing, click here.
4. FutureScope












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by FutureScope (@futurescope.dome)



Hong Kong’s first large-scale art dome, FutureScope, debuted at Kai Tak Sports Park this month. Situated outdoors near Victoria Harbour, the hemispherical theatre will immerse visitors in 360-degree sight and sound.
FutureScope will house three immersive multidisciplinary experiences from December 2025 to April 2026, titled Perpetual Records, Resonance Aura and Waving Script, featuring Hong Kong media artist Chris Cheung and his collectives Xcept and Xceed with international creators like Japanese new media artist Daito Manabe and experimental vocalist Sainkho Namtchylak.
The exhibitions will be free and open daily from 2pm to 8pm, with extended hours on weekends and public holidays from 11am to 10pm. Performance tickets and scheduling can be accessed here.
5. Pass and Goal


The prospect of Pass and Goal is not immediately appealing. A football-themed comedy with an old-fashioned premise about staying true to oneself, it features a modest cast that includes several inexperienced child actors and is directed by the filmmaker behind abysmal comedies like Lucky Fat Man (2017) and Love Detective (2015).
Yet, to echo a key message from Jil Wong Pak-kei’s latest film, a match is seldom won or lost in the first half. And so it proves here, as this feel-good, family-friendly offering defies expectations with its infectious positivity and cheeky humour.
While Gigi Leung Wing-kei, hamming it up as an egoist who must rediscover her moral compass, displays a genuine sense of humour that is not always apparent in her comedy outings, it is newcomer Chau Tsz-yuet – a real-life member of the Hong Kong women’s under-16 squad – who proves the real find as the teenage protagonist.
Some may even call Pass and Goal one of the most surprisingly wholesome Hong Kong films in recent memory. Read our full review
Pass and Goal is now showing in Hong Kong cinemas.

data.title: South Korea’s ex-president Yoon faces 10-year jail over first of 4 failed coup trials
data.description:
Prosecutors on Friday demanded a 10-year prison sentence for former South Korean president Yoon Suk-yeol in the first of four trials linked to his failed attempt to impose martial law, as a potential ruling against Yoon next month could keep him behind bars even before his most serious case is concluded.
This first case focuses on Yoon’s actions before and after the martial law decree was issued on December 3 last year, including obstructing his arrest, bypassing Cabinet procedures and forging official documents to create the appearance of a lawful proclamation.
Wearing a dark blue suit without a tie, Yoon appeared tense in court, at one point letting out a bitter laugh of disbelief as special prosecutors laid out their argument for severe punishment ahead of the scheduled verdict on January 16.
If convicted, the 65-year-old former president is expected to face at least five years in prison on the charges in this trial alone. He faces a more serious, separate charge of masterminding the failed imposition of martial law – a crime punishable by either death or life imprisonment, according to legal analysts.
“In light of the sentencing request and usual court practices, Yoon is likely to receive at least five years in prison in this trial alone, which would effectively eliminate the possibility of his release when the initial six-month custody period expires on January 18,” Yi Zoon-il, a professor at Korea University Law School, told This Week in Asia.
Yoon and his defence team has urged the 35th criminal court panel, led by senior judge Baek Dae-hyun, to postpone a verdict on the current case until after the conclusion of deliberations by another panel in February in the main insurrection case, arguing that an earlier ruling could prejudice the more serious trial.

Baek rejected the request, announcing that he would deliver a verdict on January 16, two days before Yoon’s six-month detention period expires, dashing hopes that the former president might be released for a second time.
Legal experts said the sequencing of verdicts could prove decisive as a guilty verdict in this first trial would significantly narrow Yoon’s scope for legal manoeuvring ahead of the main insurrection ruling.
In their sentencing application against Yoon, the special prosecutors said: “The defendant’s actions seriously damaged the legal order of the Republic of Korea and deeply wounded citizens who trusted him enough to elect him president. The defendant never expressed remorse or apologised to the public during the investigation and trial. Instead, he repeatedly insisted on [what he considered] the legitimacy of the martial law declaration and disputed the legality of his arrest.”
In January, Yoon fiercely resisted arrest and barricaded himself inside the fortified presidential office while being shielded by rifle-carrying security service agents. Although he was released in March on technical grounds, he was rearrested in July.
Since then, Yoon has remained defiant, maintaining what prosecutors describe as baseless claims of innocence and asserting that the martial law decree was intended to “awaken” the public to threats posed by vaguely defined pro-North Korea, anti-state forces.

Special prosecutors characterised Yoon’s conduct as “grave offences in which the defendant privatised state institutions to conceal and justify his own wrongdoing”.
They said: “Strict accountability is required to restore the constitutional order and rule of law damaged by the defendant and to prevent abuses of power by the nation’s highest authority from recurring in Korean history.”
Prosecutors have requested five years in prison for Yoon on charges of obstructing his arrest and a combined three years for infringing on Cabinet members’ constitutional rights to deliberate and vote on the martial law proclamation.
They said Yoon rushed to declare martial law without convening a full Cabinet meeting and later spread false information to foreign media claiming that troops did not block lawmakers from gathering at the National Assembly to overturn the decree.
An additional two-year sentence was sought for forging official documents related to the martial law proclamation.
This case marks the first of four insurrection-related trials involving Yoon to reach the sentencing stage, and observers have said it may serve as a gauge of the outcome of the remaining cases.

Arguments from the prosecution and the defence in the main insurrection trial – the central case concerning the martial law imposition – will conclude on January 9, with a verdict expected in February.
Yoon is also standing trial on allegations that he ordered drones to be sent to Pyongyang to provoke a security crisis and fabricate justification for declaring martial law.
Some of the drones dispatched in October and November last year reportedly crashed in North Korea with their flight data intact. The alleged transfer of classified military information to the enemy is an illegal act amounting to aiding adversaries under South Korean law.
Prosecutors further accused Yoon of selectively convening Cabinet members loyal to him to create the appearance of a legitimate Cabinet meeting.
He also faces charges of fabricating a proclamation to falsely suggest that the decree had been properly countersigned by then-prime minister Han Duck-soo and former defence minister Kim Yong-hyun.
The document was later shredded and discarded, violating laws requiring the preservation of presidential records and official documents.
Beyond the courtroom, political turmoil stemming from the failed martial law bid continues to roil South Korea.

The ruling liberal Democratic Party of Korea (DPK), which returned to power following Yoon’s impeachment in April, said on Friday that it would push for a second round of special counsel investigations combining three probes: Yoon’s martial law attempt, scandals involving his wife Kim Keon-hee, and the death of a young marine – an incident the Yoon administration is accused of attempting to cover up.
DPK leader Jung Chung-rae has pledged that the bill would be the first to be introduced by the party next year.

data.title: As India’s US$70 million obesity drug market bulges, so does price war
data.description:
Global pharmaceutical giants Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk are scrambling to cement their lead in India’s booming obesity drug market before cheaper generic versions hit shelves ‌in March next year.
Novo’s strategy emphasises price cuts and accelerated launches, while Lilly’s products benefited from hitting the market early. Both companies focused on aggressive outreach to doctors, heavier advertising about obesity, tie-ups with clinics, patient incentives and ‍distribution deals with local drug makers, according to doctors, analysts, medical representatives, patients and distributors.
Lilly has even teamed up in India with well-known Bollywood actors in a social media ad campaign about obesity.
India, projected to have the world’s second-largest overweight or obese population by 2050 in absolute numbers, is becoming a major battleground for obesity drugs. Analysts expect the global market for such drugs to hit US$150 billion a year by the end of this decade.
Although the US remains the largest market for obesity drugs, early sales figures in India show rapid uptake, even though most patients in ⁠the world’s most populous nation pay for the medication out-of-pocket.
“We believe that this market can be more than US$1 billion within two years,” said Shrikant Akolkar, vice-president at research firm Nuvama Institutional Equities.
Data analytics firm Pharmarack said in July that the market was estimated to be worth 6.28 billion rupees (US$70.23 million) at present, growing fivefold since 2021.
US drug maker Lilly’s Mounjaro, approved for diabetes and weight loss in India, became the top-selling therapy by value in October, with sales doubling within months of its March launch, outpacing Danish drug maker Novo’s Wegovy, which entered the Indian market in June.
“We realised just after a couple of months that for accessibility, we had to take a price cut,” said Vikrant Shrotriya, Novo Nordisk’s managing director in India, referring to Wegovy’s price cut in November. Shrotriya spoke earlier ‍this month while launching Novo Nordisk’s blockbuster diabetes drug, Ozempic, in the country.

Ozempic, a once-weekly injection approved by the US drug regulator in 2017 for type 2 diabetes, became a global bestseller and is widely used off-label for weight loss due to its appetite-suppressing effects.
More than ‍20 Indian drug makers, including Dr Reddy’s, Cipla, Sun Pharma, Zydus and Lupin, plan to launch cheaper versions of Novo’s weight-loss drug in India once its patent on semaglutide, the active ingredient in Wegovy and Ozempic, expires in March 2026.
Analysts expect generic drugs to cost about 60 per cent less, intensifying the fight – particularly for Novo – for dominance in India’s price-sensitive market.
Shrotriya played down concerns over looming patent expiries, saying that Novo will focus on quality, trust and affordability rather than on patents or competition in India.
Lilly’s Mounjaro has quickly gained traction in India. A 2.5mg Mounjaro KwikPen costs about 13,125 rupees (US$146.79) for a month’s usage, with the highest ‍15mg dose going up to 25,781 rupees (US$288.33).
In response, Novo cut the price of Wegovy in India by up to 37 per cent in November, pricing its lowest dose of 0.25mg at 10,850 rupees (US$121.34) for a one-month pack. It launched Ozempic last week for a monthly price of 8,800 rupees (US$98.42) for 0.25mg.
While Wegovy is catching up, Mounjaro’s early launch helped it penetrate the market, and the latter drug’s claim of offering greater weight loss makes it a popular choice among patients, according to five doctors.
“Mounjaro clearly has first-strike advantage and continues to have strong demand, but price-sensitive patients are reassessing alternatives,” said Dr Anoop Misra, an endocrinologist and ‌executive ⁠chairman of Fortis C-DOC Hospital.


Mounjaro also differentiates itself by targeting people with severe obesity, said Vishal Manchanda, analyst at Systematix Institutional Equities. He added that Lilly faces no immediate pressure to cut prices, given its strong position.
Even if Lilly lowers prices later, Mounjaro would still cost about 30 per cent more than other branded weight-loss drugs, he said.
Lilly declined to comment on its Indian pricing strategy. It said, however, that early response to Mounjaro in India has been “highly encouraging”.
Ozempic, Wegovy and Mounjaro belong to a class of drugs called GLP-1 agonists, which mimic a hormone that slows digestion and helps people feel full longer.
Novo has sued Dr Reddy’s and Sun Pharma in a local court to enforce semaglutide patents and attempted to block generics until March 2026.
Longer patent protection for ‌Mounjaro’s active ingredient, tirzepatide, which extends at least until the middle of the next decade, also gives Lilly an advantage, analysts said.
The battle has expanded beyond pricing and patents.

Demand for weight-loss treatments in India is spreading beyond urban elites as interest grows among middle-class families, office workers, women nearing menopause and people with obesity-related complications in smaller towns.
Lilly has partnered with India’s third-largest drug maker by revenue, Cipla, to launch a second tirzepatide brand, Yurpeak, ‌targeting smaller towns, and teamed up with Apollo Hospitals to raise awareness ‌of obesity and diabetes. It is also investing more than US$1 billion to expand contract manufacturing in India.
Cipla said it would help market Lilly’s weight-loss medication in deeper markets in India.
Meanwhile, Novo has tied up with Emcure Pharmaceuticals and launched a second semaglutide brand, Poviztra, to widen distribution beyond India’s major cities. It has teamed with start-up Healthify to offer patients health coaching and with Apollo to raise awareness of obesity.
In India, where prescription drug ads are prohibited, Novo launched a “WeGoWithYou” obesity awareness campaign that connects people to doctors to learn more about the disease and its management. Lilly has a similar initiative known as “WeKnowNow” that talks about obesity management.
Both drug makers are pushing to frame obesity as a disease, utilising newspaper ads, billboards, airport displays and pamphlets at wellness clinics.
Neither company disclosed its spending on marketing in India.

data.title: Beijing extends Mars mission lead; stranded astronauts back home: China’s year in space
data.description:
Catch up on some of SCMP’s biggest China space stories of the year. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. Stranded Chinese astronauts return home from space station mission
Three astronauts whose return to Earth from China’s Tiangong space station was delayed after a suspected debris strike touched down on November 14 at the Dongfeng landing site in the Inner Mongolia autonomous region.
2. China’s Shenzhou 20 debris crisis: a catalyst for space war – or peace?
A tiny piece of debris triggered the most serious mishap at China’s Tiangong space station since it became operational three years ago.
3. China’s Mars mission leaves US for space dust with Nasa at least 4 years behind

China’s lead in the race to bring rocks back from Mars has grown by a big margin, as Nasa’s updated timeline for the return of samples collected by its Perseverance rover puts it at least four years behind.
4. China’s satellite spies on US spycraft in a ‘tit-for-tat’ orbital stand-off
In a notable display of reconnaissance capabilities, a Chinese satellite has observed an American satellite that was itself monitoring China’s space station and high-value satellite, a move analysts described as a strategic tit-for-tat in an increasingly contested space domain.
5. US scientists find ‘critical gaps’ in Nasa plan to beat China on the moon
Scientists have found critical gaps in Nasa’s development of space life support systems that could prevent the United States from competing with China in the pursuit of long-term manned space exploration and habitation.
6. New strain of bacteria found on China’s Tiangong space station

Chinese scientists have discovered a new microbe that developed on board the county’s Tiangong space station, according to a new study.
7. China’s first reusable rocket Zhuque-3 fails recovery, leaving US in the lead
China’s first reusable rocket, the Zhuque-3, launched for the first time on December 3 and successfully reached orbit, but efforts to recover the first stage failed after it crashed near the planned recovery site.
8. China makes first 5G satellite-to-phone video call. Will it defy TikTok curbs?
The world’s first direct 5G satellite-to-smartphone broadband video call by scientists in China has raised new questions about the effectiveness of US restrictions on apps like TikTok.
9. US space agency Nasa will not fund study on China’s moon sample, says scientist

American scientists are eager to get their hands on China’s Chang’e-5 moon samples – but they will not be allowed to use Nasa funding to study them.
10. China launches satellites to build the world’s first supercomputer in orbit
China has launched the first batch of satellites for its space computing constellation, a system that could rival the most powerful ground-based supercomputers once fully deployed.
11. China plans to build ‘Three Gorges dam in space’ to harness solar power
A senior Chinese scientist has revealed an ambitious plan to use super heavy rockets to build solar power stations in space, calling it “another Three Gorges Dam project above the Earth”.
12. China-led study finds sign of liquid water in Mars
Liquid water may be locked in rock just a few kilometres beneath Martian ground – much closer to the surface of the red planet than previously thought.
Passionate about science? Dive deeper with the Dark Matters newsletter, a weekly in-depth analysis on China’s rise in science, technology and military that goes beneath the surface. Sign up for free now.

data.title: How Chinese artist Xu Beihong’s horse paintings have galloped through history
data.description:
From auspicious horse-themed phrases and couplets to whether your luck is in, check out our Year of the Horse 2026 series to discover all you need to know about the coming Lunar New Year.
In Chinese culture, horses often symbolise power, vitality and ambition. But in the eyes of Xu Beihong – the father of modern Chinese art, who was especially revered for his horse paintings – the strong animal represented something solemn, persistent and even tragic.
Xu was born in 1895, after the end of the first Sino-Japanese war, and he witnessed intense changes in China throughout his lifetime, including the end of imperial China, Japanese aggression, World War II and the rise of the Communist Party. His work, therefore, reflected the country’s turbulent transformation in the first half of the 20th century.
His early family life, too, was one of turmoil. He grew up in rural Yixing, in Jiangsu province, where he helped out on the family farm. His father, a teacher, taught him calligraphy from the age of six and Chinese painting from the age of nine.

During his teenage years, because of frequent flooding in his hometown, he often journeyed with his father to nearby counties, where they made a living by selling their paintings. Xu painted many naturalist subjects during this time.
When Xu was 17, his father fell ill, which meant his family lost their primary income. As a result, Xu returned to Yixing to teach art in schools. His parents arranged a marriage for him, which he tried to resist by running away – to no avail.
At 18, he fathered his first son, who died of smallpox at the age of seven in the boy’s mother’s hometown. When Xu was 19, his father died, and – so it has been said – Xu had to borrow money for his burial from his father’s close friend.
These hardships in his formative years led to a name change that occurred sometime in the 1920s, from Shoukang, meaning longevity and health, to Beihong, meaning a sorrowful migratory goose.
In 1916, at 21, Xu enrolled in the French department at Shanghai’s Fudan University. He painted, made connections in the art circle and was soon invited to teach at Meiji University in Tokyo. In 1917, he went to Japan to study art.

This was a time of much unrest in China. In 1912, imperial rule ended and, for years after, the newly established Republic of China remained politically fragmented. Meanwhile, Japan asserted its dominance in parts of China, particularly in the northeast.
Xu was inspired by, and contributed to, the progressive New Culture Movement in China during the 1910s and 1920s.
He once said: “Of the traditional techniques, one should preserve the outstanding ones, inherit the disappearing ones, change the inadequate ones and infuse the adaptable Western techniques into one’s work.”
Between 1919 and 1927, he travelled to France, where he learned Western art techniques at the Ecole Nationale Superieure des Beaux-Arts in Paris and studied under notable painters such as Francois Flameng, Fernand Cormon and Pascal Dagnan-Bouveret. He made oil paintings during this time and sketched extensively.
He travelled throughout western Europe, where he frequently visited local zoos, and his works were shown at the Salon – the official exhibition of art sponsored by the French government – in 1923 and 1927.
When he returned to China, ink, colour and paper became his primary media. He became famed for a style that integrated Western academic realism – oil painting and life sketching – with traditional Chinese art.

Xu, unlike many Chinese artists who focused on imitating the old masters, embraced the French realism movement and Western methodology, adding real-life observations and expressions into his work and promoting a new and modernised form of Chinese art.
One of his highest-priced works, Put Down Your Whip, is a 1939 oil painting inspired by an anti-Japanese street drama performance. It auctioned for HK$72 million in 2007, a world record for a Chinese painting at the time.
However, his prolific oeuvre is best known for his expressions of horses with swift brushstrokes and thick lines that embodied fuller, more urgent feelings.
Xu’s most famous ink-on-paper horse painting titles include: Galloping Horse, Running Horse, Standing Horse, The Three Stallions, Two Horses and Horse Drinking Water.
He sometimes repeated these titles, which created numerous versions of these paintings, but they have all been described as having different characteristics and personalities. Sometimes, it is a quiet dignity against a mostly barren backdrop; other times, they are bold and unrestrained, reflecting a certain defiance during social upheavals.

A 1942 Running Horse sold for HK$7.2 million at Christie’s Hong Kong in November 2014, while a 1947 Galloping Horse fetched US$437,000 at Christie’s New York in September 2016.
Twelve years ago, during the previous Year of the Horse, an exhibition of his work was held in Hong Kong. His son, Xu Qingping, said there was “an air of tragedy” that made the steeds’ lean, muscular and military bodies different from “the horses of today”.
He added that the horse paintings told of the difficult times in which his father lived, as the younger Xu also painted stallions, but his were more relaxed and “fat” than the horses his father painted.
A lesser-known horse painting by Xu Beihong, titled Sick Horse (1941), “exuded an air of melancholy and dejection”, wrote a Post reporter in 2008.
The story goes that Loh Cheng Chuan, a collector of Chinese art who was a friend of Xu’s in Penang, Malaysia, was pondering the matters of old age and sickness and asked Xu to paint an ailing animal for him.
Xu agreed and accompanied the painting with a poem explaining that the weakened stallion was a metaphor for his own tough years in Europe as a struggling artist.

Kwok Kian Chow, the former director of the Singapore Art Museum, said in 2008 that Xu’s name was “one of the first names to be mentioned” when considering the topic of realism in Asian modern art, and that his style was “predicated on the ocular world as opposed to the literati tradition of text”.
Kwok added that Xu’s work had “an immense influence on the development of Chinese painting in the 20th century because he championed an expansive realism that included Romanticism and Expressionism”.

data.title: Hong Kong is emerging as the world’s AI crossroads between East and West: PwC
data.description:
Hong Kong is emerging as a neutral hub bridging the technological strengths of East and West while leveraging its robust education and financial systems to drive the next wave of artificial intelligence transformation in businesses, according to PwC executives.
As companies worldwide pushed to move AI from isolated experiments to capabilities deployed at scale, not just to solve problems but to unlock new revenue opportunities, Hong Kong was “well‑positioned” to harness the technology’s potential, said Joe Atkinson, chief AI officer at PwC Global.
“Hong Kong serves as a powerful connector, bringing together capabilities from around the world to address business challenges in industrial AI and supply chains,” he said in an interview with the Post earlier this month. “Its education system, financial institutions and business environment all play an important role in driving AI transformation.”
With AI promising increases in efficiency and productivity, as well as driving innovation, creativity and scientific discovery, the city could become a research and development hub that combines the strengths of East and West, according to Vishy Narayanan, digital and AI leader at PwC Asia-Pacific, in the same interview.

China has surged alongside the US, European Union and Japan in the global AI race, propelled by breakthroughs such as DeepSeek’s low-cost, highly efficient models, as well as national backing, according to a recent report by think tank Deep Knowledge Group.
Hong Kong, leveraging its proximity to mainland China and support from Beijing, was positioned as an international hub for AI-driven innovation, said King Au King-lun, executive director of the city’s Financial Services Development Council (FSDC), which contributed to the report.
Hong Kong’s AI sector included 500 organisations, 290 companies and 183 investors active in finance, healthcare and government services, according to another study by Deep Knowledge Group, the Artificial Intelligence Association of Hong Kong and FSDC. The sector was supported by 27 key innovation hubs that included universities, Hong Kong Science and Technology Parks Corporation, HKAI Lab and Cyberport, home to more than 1,800 digital tech companies.
Hong Kong’s policy direction helped energise the city’s business community, Atkinson said.
“The focus on resilience and tokenisation in Hong Kong’s 2030 FinTech plan is remarkably forward‑looking,” he said.
The five-year fintech strategy, announced last month by the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA), expanded the conversation beyond technology to consider the business operations, impact and structural changes needed for an AI‑native world, Atkinson added. “That’s why we’re seeing such a bullish response from businesses in the region – they recognise and support this forward‑leaning vision.”
The city’s de facto central bank plans to launch more than 40 initiatives to embed AI in finance, build a financial tokenisation ecosystem, create data and payment infrastructure, and enhance sector-wide resilience. For example, the HKMA has begun facilitating real-value transactions using tokenised deposits and digital assets under a controlled pilot programme.
Meanwhile, over 90 per cent of the city’s flagship supercomputing centre in Cyberport was in use, supplying high-end computing resources to universities, research institutions and industry players.
“In Asia-Pacific, new AI technologies have reached markets only months after launching in the US or UK, giving the region time to learn from early adopters and take a more proactive stance on regulation around data, ethics and explainability,” Narayanan said.
“Markets such as Singapore and Hong Kong are going further by embedding talent development into AI policy, while others are beginning to explore the intersection between AI and sustainability.”
Organisations adopting AI were growing revenue per employee at three times the rate of others, according to PwC’s global AI jobs barometer report. “Those bringing their people along on the journey are seeing stronger top‑line results, as AI shifts from a competitive differentiator to a business necessity,” Atkinson said.
“AI adoption ultimately depends on people – empowering and incentivising the workforce to embrace it confidently,” Narayanan added.

data.title: China village imposes ‘unjust’ fines for out-of-province marriage and cohabitation
data.description:
A Chinese village has faced an online backlash after it introduced controversial fines for pregnancies outside marriage and couples living together before they wed.
The village, in Lincang, Yunnan province, southwestern China, sparked heated debate online after a notice titled “Village Rules: Everyone is Equal” was photographed and reposted by netizens.
According to widely circulated photos on social media, the notice outlines strict penalties for unmarried pregnancies, cohabitation without marriage and even marrying someone from outside Yunnan province.

It states: “Marrying someone from outside the province requires a penalty of 1,500 yuan (US$210); unmarried pregnancy 3,000 yuan; and unmarried couples living together must pay 500 yuan (US$70) annually.”
Other stipulations included: “If a child is born too soon after marriage, less than 10 months, a fine of 3,000 yuan will be imposed” and “If a couple quarrels and village officials are called in to mediate, each person will be fined 500 yuan”.
Also, people who cause trouble or display rowdy behaviour while drinking in other villages will face fines ranging from 3,000 to 5,000 yuan (US$700).
Those caught spreading rumours or making baseless claims within the village will be fined between 500 and 1,000 yuan.

The population size and economic level of the village are unclear.
On December 16, an official from Mengding Town Government told Red Star News that the content of the notice was “very unusual” and that it has since been taken down.
The official also explained that the notice had been put up by the village committee on its own initiative, without reporting or submitting it for approval to the township government.
They also said that there are no local regulations prohibiting interprovincial or interethnic marriages.
The notice triggered a severe backlash online.
One person said: “They must be crazy for money.”
While another asked: “Are we in 1925 or 2025? A village like this is no place for normal people to live.”

“Pretty good for generating revenue, huh? Did the fines go to the village officials for drinking?” asked a third.
While a fourth person added: “Is this not outright extortion? It is just too outrageous.”
In 2023, the government in Puge county, Sichuan province, southwestern China made headlines for imposing penalties on residents who failed to complete basic household chores.
The rules included a 10-yuan (US$1) fine for not making your bed or leaving dishes unwashed and a 20-yuan fine for squatting while eating.
